name: Fresno Unified School District
districtFact: The largest school district in California's Central Valley and the fourth largest in the state. Founded in 1885, Fresno Unified has grown alongside the San Joaquin Valley's agricultural economy into one of California's most diverse urban school systems.
about: Fresno Unified School District is the fourth largest school district in California, serving approximately 70,000 students across 100 schools in the Central Valley. The district offers Dual Language Immersion programs in Spanish and Hmong, Career and Technical Education pathways, and one of California's most established Agricultural Education programs — reflecting the San Joaquin Valley's identity as the most productive farming region in the United States.
planningTips: Fresno Unified begins in mid-August — 3–4 weeks earlier than LAUSD and most coastal California districts. Plan back-to-school preparations for late July to avoid the heat peak and ensure enrollment, supplies, and childcare are arranged before the first day.

From September through October, wildfire smoke from Sierra Nevada fires can push AQI into unhealthy ranges. Check AirNow on days with outdoor school activities planned. If your child has asthma, ensure their rescue inhaler prescription is current before wildfire season begins.

Lincoln's Birthday (observed around February 12) is a paid school holiday in Fresno Unified — a California-specific holiday not observed in most other states. Mark it on your calendar for childcare planning well before the school year begins.

Cesar Chavez Day (March 31) is a California state school holiday. In years when it falls outside Spring Break, it becomes an additional no-school day — confirm each year against the official district calendar.

Fresno Unified schedules several District Buyback Days throughout the year — days when teachers attend professional development while students stay home. These are published on the official calendar in August. Add them to your childcare calendar before the school year starts.

Dense Tule fog typically develops from November through February and can cause school bus delays or modified schedules with little warning. Sign up for Fresno Unified's parent notification system before November to receive real-time alerts. Also confirm your school emergency contacts are up to date at the start of the year — fog-related delays may require early morning childcare changes.

Fresno is 65 miles from Yosemite's South Entrance. Yosemite Valley day-use reservations open on a 28-day rolling window — to visit during Spring Break, set a calendar reminder about 28 days before your target date so you can book the moment reservations open. Weekend reservations can fill quickly; weekday slots are generally easier to secure.

districtFaqs.q: Why does Fresno Unified start school so early compared to other California districts?
districtFaqs.a: Fresno Unified's early start is driven by the Central Valley's extreme summer heat. Beginning in mid-August allows the school year to end before the most intense heat of June, when temperatures regularly exceed 105°F. Temperatures can exceed 110°F during heat waves in July and August, making the earlier end-of-year a meaningful quality-of-life factor for families. The calendar structure also aligns with local agricultural and community rhythms in the San Joaquin Valley.

districtFaqs.q: What is a District Buyback Day in Fresno Unified?
districtFaqs.a: Buyback Days (also called Staff Development Days or Teacher Workdays) are days when teachers and staff report to school for professional development, training, or planning — but students do not attend. These are typically scattered throughout the school year and are listed in the official Fresno Unified calendar.

districtFaqs.q: Is Fresno Unified the same as Fresno State?
districtFaqs.a: No. Fresno Unified School District (FUSD) is the K-12 public school system serving the city of Fresno. California State University, Fresno (Fresno State) is a separate university with its own academic calendar. Fresno State's semester schedule does not affect Fresno Unified school dates.

districtFaqs.q: Why is Lincoln's Birthday a holiday in Fresno Unified?
districtFaqs.a: California state law designates Abraham Lincoln's Birthday (February 12) as a paid holiday for school employees. Not all California districts close schools on this date, but many Central Valley districts, including Fresno Unified, observe it. This holiday is specific to California and is not recognized in most other states.

districtFaqs.q: How do I apply for Fresno Unified's specialty programs?
districtFaqs.a: Fresno Unified's school choice enrollment window typically opens in the fall for the following school year. Families interested in Dual Language Immersion (offered in Spanish and Hmong), Career and Technical Education pathways, or Agricultural Education programs should contact the district's school choice office by October or November — some programs close applications before December. Enrollment in specialty programs is not automatic; families must apply separately from their neighborhood school assignment. Check the district website in September for the current-year choice enrollment timeline.

districtFaqs.q: What should I know about Tule fog and the school year?
districtFaqs.a: Tule fog is a dense ground fog that forms in the San Joaquin Valley from November through February. It can reduce visibility to near zero overnight and cause school bus delays, staggered arrival times, or school closures. Unlike snow days, there is no accumulation to monitor — Tule fog can appear and lift unpredictably. Fresno Unified communicates fog-related schedule changes through its parent notification system. Families should ensure their contact information is current before November so they receive real-time alerts.

livingHere.intro: Fresno sits at the geographic center of California's San Joaquin Valley, surrounded by the most productive agricultural land in the United States. For families relocating here, the city's rhythms are shaped less by coastal trends and more by the land, climate, and economy that define Central Valley life.

highlights.label: Extreme Summer Heat
highlights.detail: Fresno regularly records temperatures above 105°F from June through August — among the hottest of any major California city. Air conditioning is essential, and outdoor afternoon activities during back-to-school weeks in late July are genuinely hazardous for young children.

highlights.label: Tule Fog Season
highlights.detail: Dense Tule fog rolls through the San Joaquin Valley from November through February, frequently reducing visibility to near zero. School bus delays and closures are common during fog advisories — fog, not snow, is the weather event Fresno families plan around each winter.

highlights.label: Agricultural Employment Cycles
highlights.detail: The Fresno economy is deeply tied to grape, citrus, almond, and stone fruit harvests. Seasonal agricultural employment peaks in summer and fall, affecting work schedules and family availability in ways that do not apply to urban districts elsewhere in California.

highlights.label: Yosemite National Park Access
highlights.detail: Yosemite's South Entrance (Wawona/Mariposa Grove) is approximately 65 miles from Fresno — roughly a 90-minute drive. Summer reservations for Yosemite Valley day visits sell out within minutes of release, often in January or February. Plan school-year break trips to Yosemite during spring or fall for better access.

highlights.label: Fresno Yosemite International Airport (FAT)
highlights.detail: FAT is a small regional airport with limited direct routes — mostly to West Coast hubs, Phoenix, and Las Vegas. Nonstop options are significantly fewer than at LAX or SFO. Most long-distance travel requires a connecting flight or a 3-hour drive to the Bay Area.

highlights.label: Air Quality and Valley Fever
highlights.detail: Fresno County has among the worst air quality in the United States, driven by agricultural dust, vehicle emissions, and wildfire smoke each fall. On high-AQI days, Fresno Unified may move outdoor PE classes indoors and shorten recess — families should enable school notifications and check AirNow before morning drop-off during fire season. Valley Fever (a fungal lung infection from soil dust) is endemic to the San Joaquin Valley; families new to the region should discuss it with their pediatrician, especially for children who spend time outdoors.
